workflow.workflow_name: RIA code-of-ethics personal trading exception review
workflow.persona: Internal product strategist mapping the code-of-ethics exception workflow used by a compliance team reviewing employee personal trading and reporting issues
workflow.entry_condition: The adviser is running a periodic code-of-ethics review cycle or has received new employee trading, holdings, acknowledgment, or preclearance data that must be checked for exceptions.
workflow.completion_condition: Exceptions are triaged, any violations or waivers are adjudicated by the CCO, and the resulting records and follow-up actions are archived.
workflow_notes: This repo still treats each workflow slice as a locked artifact, not a multi-workflow product surface.
This third slice stays inside RIA compliance but changes the work shape again from filing and marketing review into recurring exception monitoring and supervisory adjudication.
Representative internal artifacts are included as workflow inputs, not as fabricated evidence.
Source evidence is limited to SEC rulemaking, risk alerts, form instructions, and examination-priority materials.
Firm-specific sanction matrices, restricted-list thresholds, and escalation routing are treated as local configuration, not unresolved scoring uncertainty.

observed_inputs.note: Source and artifact inventories are observed or curated workflow inputs. Scores and recommendations are inferred from those inputs.

dimensions.label: Automation fit
dimensions.description: Higher means the step is structured, rules-based, and technically repeatable.

dimensions.label: Human sign-off need
dimensions.description: Higher means a named human owner should retain final authority for the step.

dimensions.label: Liability asymmetry
dimensions.description: Higher means the downside of a wrong answer is materially larger than the efficiency gain.

dimensions.label: Evidence strength
dimensions.description: Higher means the step can be anchored in explicit source material, artifacts, and traceable checks.

source_inventory.title: Investment Adviser MNPI Compliance Issues
source_inventory.summary: Describes common code-of-ethics deficiencies, including incomplete access-person identification, missing pre-approval controls, weak review evidence, missing acknowledgments, and employee trading in restricted securities.

source_inventory.title: Final Rule: Investment Adviser Codes of Ethics
source_inventory.summary: Adopted rule 204A-1 requiring adviser codes of ethics, periodic access-person holdings and transaction reports, pre-approval for IPOs and limited offerings, written acknowledgments, and related recordkeeping.

source_inventory.title: SEC Division of Examinations Announces 2026 Priorities
source_inventory.summary: Says examinations will continue to assess the effectiveness of adviser compliance programs, whether policies are implemented and enforced, and whether annual compliance reviews address core conflict and trading risks.

source_inventory.title: Appendix C Part 2 of Form ADV
source_inventory.summary: Requires advisers to describe their code of ethics, offer a copy to clients on request, and explain conflicts created by adviser or related-person personal trading in the same securities recommended to clients.

source_inventory.title: The Five Most Frequent Compliance Topics Identified in OCIE Examinations of Investment Advisers
source_inventory.summary: Identifies the code-of-ethics rule as a frequent examination topic, including failures to identify all access persons and failures to collect or review required holdings and transaction reports on time.

source_inventory.title: Compliance Alert (July 2008)
source_inventory.summary: Highlights common personal-trading deficiencies and effective practices such as duplicate confirms, current restricted lists, documented preclearance, centralized monitoring, annual certifications, and documented exceptions.

artifact_inventory.name: Access-person roster and role matrix
artifact_inventory.description: Roster of supervised persons, role attributes, related entities, and current access-person designation logic.

artifact_inventory.name: Brokerage feed and duplicate-confirm map
artifact_inventory.description: Mapping of employee brokerage accounts, duplicate-confirm delivery, feed coverage, and known manual-reporting gaps.

artifact_inventory.name: Client trade blotter comparison snapshot
artifact_inventory.description: Selected client-order and allocation data used to compare flagged employee trades against client trading activity and blackout rules.

artifact_inventory.name: Code delivery and acknowledgment log
artifact_inventory.description: Log of code distributions, amendment notices, written acknowledgments, and annual certification status.

artifact_inventory.name: Code-of-ethics exception triage queue
artifact_inventory.description: Structured list of missing reports, late acknowledgments, restricted-list hits, unmatched trades, and other flagged exceptions.

artifact_inventory.name: Exception review memo and remediation draft
artifact_inventory.description: Reviewer draft summarizing the facts, likely policy issue, remediation options, training needs, and any disclosure follow-up.

artifact_inventory.name: IPO, limited-offering, and preclearance request log
artifact_inventory.description: Requests, approvals, denials, expiration dates, and supporting notes for personal-trading preclearance decisions.

artifact_inventory.name: Restricted-list and watchlist register
artifact_inventory.description: Current do-not-trade, watchlist, and blackout-period controls used to screen employee trading activity.

artifact_inventory.name: Quarterly transaction and annual holdings package
artifact_inventory.description: Combined set of quarterly transaction reports, annual holdings reports, initial holdings reports, and supporting account statements.

artifact_inventory.name: Violation, sanction, and retention log
artifact_inventory.description: Final record of violations, waivers, sanctions, remediation, annual certifications, and five-year retention metadata.

summary.first_build_wedge: Automate the monitoring rails, assist the conflict-heavy exception review, and keep final violation adjudication human.

highest_priority_build_step.step_name: Reconcile missing, late, and incomplete reports

lowest_confidence_step.step_name: Draft the exception memo and remediation package

summary.blocked_pattern: Do not ship autonomous violation adjudication, sanctions assignment, or waiver approval for this workflow in slice 3.

review_queue.action: Keep reviewer confirmation prominent because the downside of a bad recommendation is still high.
Keep reviewer confirmation prominent because the downside of a bad recommendation is still high.
Keep any future UI limited to approval support and audit traceability, not autonomous action.

step_results.step_description: Collect the access-person roster, broker-feed coverage, report package, and acknowledgment log into one normalized packet before exception scoring begins.

artifacts.name: Quarterly transaction and annual holdings package

automation_fit.label: Automation fit
automation_fit.rationale: Packet assembly is recurring, checklist-driven work that can normalize the same record types each cycle.

human_signoff_need.label: Human sign-off need
human_signoff_need.rationale: A reviewer should inspect gaps, but this intake step does not decide whether conduct violated the code.

liability_asymmetry.label: Liability asymmetry
liability_asymmetry.rationale: An incomplete packet creates rework and downstream review risk, but it is still an early-stage control rather than the final disciplinary call.

evidence_strength.label: Evidence strength
evidence_strength.rationale: The input set is directly anchored to rule-level reporting, acknowledgment, and recordkeeping obligations.

evidence_notes.source_title: Final Rule: Investment Adviser Codes of Ethics
evidence_notes.claim: Rule 204A-1 and the recordkeeping amendments require advisers to collect and review access-person reports and acknowledgments, which supports a standardized intake packet.

evidence_notes.source_title: Compliance Alert (July 2008)
evidence_notes.claim: SEC staff observed stronger programs where duplicate confirms, brokerage statements, and centralized monitoring were already pulled into one compliance process.

evidence_notes.source_title: Investment Adviser MNPI Compliance Issues
evidence_notes.claim: Exams cited missing review evidence and missing acknowledgments, so packet completeness reduces the risk of missing required follow-up later in the cycle.

evidence_notes.source_title: SEC Division of Examinations Announces 2026 Priorities
evidence_notes.claim: Current examinations still test whether compliance programs are implemented and enforced, which favors a documented intake control even though the packet owner is not the final decision-maker.

step_results.build_implication: Build a repeatable intake rail that assembles one review packet and exposes missing inputs before any human exception analysis starts.

confidence_breakdown.note: Clear score separation supports the current recommendation.

decision_rationale.why_this_mode: Automate is the best fit because automation fit (82) and evidence strength (86) comfortably outweigh human sign-off need (30) and liability asymmetry (30).
decision_rationale.why_not_more_human: Human review should stay focused on exceptions, not default packet handling.
decision_rationale.confidence_note: Clear score separation supports the current recommendation.

step_results.step_name: Confirm access-person scope and reporting obligations
step_results.step_description: Review the role matrix, related entities, and account coverage to confirm who is an access person, which accounts are reportable, and which cases need explicit reviewer judgment.

automation_fit.label: Automation fit
automation_fit.rationale: Core definitions are rule-based, but edge cases depend on firm structure, role design, and access to recommendations or nonpublic information.

human_signoff_need.label: Human sign-off need
human_signoff_need.rationale: Someone accountable for the code should confirm edge-role classification because misclassifying access persons creates surveillance blind spots.

liability_asymmetry.label: Liability asymmetry
liability_asymmetry.rationale: If the wrong people are scoped out, required reports and preclearance obligations can be missed for an extended period.

evidence_strength.label: Evidence strength
evidence_strength.rationale: The rule text and exam findings provide clear anchors for who should be supervised and why this control matters.

evidence_notes.source_title: Investment Adviser MNPI Compliance Issues
evidence_notes.claim: EXAMS observed advisers that failed to identify and supervise certain employees as access persons, making classification accuracy a real compliance risk.

evidence_notes.source_title: Final Rule: Investment Adviser Codes of Ethics
evidence_notes.claim: The adopting release ties access-person status to access to client recommendations, holdings, and other nonpublic information, which makes named reviewer judgment important for borderline roles.

evidence_notes.source_title: The Five Most Frequent Compliance Topics Identified in OCIE Examinations of Investment Advisers
evidence_notes.claim: The SEC highlighted failures to identify all access persons as a frequent code-of-ethics exam issue, reinforcing the downside of over-automating edge classifications.

evidence_notes.source_title: SEC Division of Examinations Announces 2026 Priorities
evidence_notes.claim: Current exam priorities still focus on whether policies are actually tailored to the adviser’s operations, which means a tool can structure the decision but should not silently decide every borderline case.

step_results.build_implication: Build a reviewer-visible access-person classifier that flags edge cases and records the basis for inclusion or exclusion instead of making hidden scope decisions.

confidence_breakdown.note: Moderate score separation supports the recommendation, but the second-best mode remains plausible.

decision_rationale.why_this_mode: Assist is the right lane because the step is partially structured (58) but still carries meaningful human sign-off need (66) and liability asymmetry (63).
decision_rationale.why_not_automate: Do not let this step resolve the review on its own; it should prepare judgment, not replace it.
decision_rationale.confidence_note: Moderate score separation supports the recommendation, but the second-best mode remains plausible.

step_results.step_name: Reconcile missing, late, and incomplete reports
step_results.step_description: Compare required holdings reports, quarterly trade reports, and acknowledgments against what was actually received and time-stamp each resulting exception.

artifacts.name: Quarterly transaction and annual holdings package

automation_fit.label: Automation fit
automation_fit.rationale: This is rule-driven exception detection against known due dates, required report types, and acknowledgment requirements.

human_signoff_need.label: Human sign-off need
human_signoff_need.rationale: A human should review escalated exceptions, but the matching of expected versus received reports is mostly mechanical.

liability_asymmetry.label: Liability asymmetry
liability_asymmetry.rationale: Missing a report exception matters, but the resulting issue can still be checked and corrected before discipline or disclosure decisions are made.

evidence_strength.label: Evidence strength
evidence_strength.rationale: The timing, content, and review obligations are explicitly described in rule and exam materials.

evidence_notes.source_title: Final Rule: Investment Adviser Codes of Ethics
evidence_notes.claim: Rule 204A-1 requires periodic holdings and transaction reporting plus adviser review, which creates a clean expected-versus-received exception check.

evidence_notes.source_title: Compliance Alert (July 2008)
evidence_notes.claim: SEC staff observed effective programs using duplicate confirms, electronic records, and reviewer controls such as not permitting the reviewer to self-review, which supports automated exception detection with human escalation.

evidence_notes.source_title: The Five Most Frequent Compliance Topics Identified in OCIE Examinations of Investment Advisers
evidence_notes.claim: Untimely, missing, or unreviewed access-person reports were highlighted as common exam deficiencies, so catching them early has clear supervisory value.

evidence_notes.source_title: Investment Adviser MNPI Compliance Issues
evidence_notes.claim: EXAMS cited late submissions and lack of evidence of supervisory review, which means an automated exception queue directly addresses a recurring deficiency pattern.

step_results.build_implication: Build a rules-driven exception engine for late reports, missing acknowledgments, and incomplete content before spending effort on a heavier reviewer experience.

confidence_breakdown.note: Clear score separation supports the current recommendation.

decision_rationale.why_this_mode: Automate is the best fit because automation fit (86) and evidence strength (88) comfortably outweigh human sign-off need (35) and liability asymmetry (38).
decision_rationale.why_not_more_human: Human review should stay focused on exceptions, not default packet handling.
decision_rationale.confidence_note: Clear score separation supports the current recommendation.

step_results.step_name: Review trades, preclearance, and restricted-list exceptions
step_results.step_description: Compare flagged employee trades and preclearance requests against restricted lists, blackout controls, and client trading context to decide whether the exception is real and material.

artifacts.name: Quarterly transaction and annual holdings package
IPO, limited-offering, and preclearance request log

automation_fit.label: Automation fit
automation_fit.rationale: The system can line up dates, lists, and reported trades, but the significance of a potential conflict still depends on context and supervisory interpretation.

human_signoff_need.label: Human sign-off need
human_signoff_need.rationale: A named reviewer should determine whether a flagged trade reflects a true code issue, an approved exception, or a harmless mismatch.

liability_asymmetry.label: Liability asymmetry
liability_asymmetry.rationale: Misreading a restricted-list or client-conflict exception can lead to overlooked fiduciary issues or unnecessary disciplinary action.

evidence_strength.label: Evidence strength
evidence_strength.rationale: The SEC sources are explicit about preclearance, restricted-list controls, and personal-trading conflict disclosure.

evidence_notes.source_title: Final Rule: Investment Adviser Codes of Ethics
evidence_notes.claim: The adopting release requires pre-approval for IPOs and limited offerings and links personal-trading controls to fiduciary obligations, which makes this review step high consequence.

evidence_notes.source_title: Compliance Alert (July 2008)
evidence_notes.claim: SEC staff observed effective programs maintaining current restricted and watch lists, enforcing blackout periods, and comparing preclearance forms to actual trading, which makes the comparison work highly structureable.

evidence_notes.source_title: Investment Adviser MNPI Compliance Issues
evidence_notes.claim: EXAMS observed employees trading restricted securities and missing pre-approval controls, so flagged trades should stay reviewer-visible rather than being auto-cleared.

evidence_notes.source_title: Appendix C Part 2 of Form ADV
evidence_notes.claim: Form ADV Part 2 requires firms to describe how they address conflicts from adviser and related-person personal trading, which raises the cost of an incorrect reviewer conclusion.

step_results.build_implication: Build a reviewer workspace that assembles restricted-list, preclearance, and client-trade context for each flag instead of auto-concluding that every hit is a violation.

confidence_breakdown.note: Moderate score separation supports the recommendation, but the second-best mode remains plausible.

decision_rationale.why_this_mode: Assist is the right lane because the step is partially structured (56) but still carries meaningful human sign-off need (72) and liability asymmetry (74).
decision_rationale.why_not_automate: Do not let this step resolve the review on its own; it should prepare judgment, not replace it.
decision_rationale.confidence_note: Moderate score separation supports the recommendation, but the second-best mode remains plausible.

review_actions: Keep reviewer confirmation prominent because the downside of a bad recommendation is still high.

step_results.step_name: Draft the exception memo and remediation package
step_results.step_description: Turn the reviewed exceptions into a reviewer-editable memo covering facts, likely policy issue, proposed remediation, training needs, and any disclosure follow-up.

artifacts.name: IPO, limited-offering, and preclearance request log
Exception review memo and remediation draft

automation_fit.label: Automation fit
automation_fit.rationale: A tool can draft the packet structure and suggested follow-ups, but the narrative still depends on how the reviewer frames the issue and remediation.

human_signoff_need.label: Human sign-off need
human_signoff_need.rationale: The memo should remain reviewer-owned because it becomes the bridge between monitoring output and formal supervisory action.

liability_asymmetry.label: Liability asymmetry
liability_asymmetry.rationale: A bad memo can understate a real problem, overstate a minor one, or miss a needed disclosure or training response.

evidence_strength.label: Evidence strength
evidence_strength.rationale: The content can be anchored in rule obligations, exam findings, and brochure disclosure requirements.

evidence_notes.source_title: Final Rule: Investment Adviser Codes of Ethics
evidence_notes.claim: The SEC requires records of code violations and actions taken, which makes the memo and remediation packet a structured supervisory output rather than optional narrative.

evidence_notes.source_title: Compliance Alert (July 2008)
evidence_notes.claim: Observed effective practices included documented exceptions, sanctions, annual certifications, and training, which gives the memo a consistent structure even when the facts vary.

evidence_notes.source_title: Investment Adviser MNPI Compliance Issues
evidence_notes.claim: The 2022 alert notes that firms modified their codes and procedures after deficiencies were identified, so the remediation package should prepare judgment rather than silently resolve the issue.

evidence_notes.source_title: Appendix C Part 2 of Form ADV
evidence_notes.claim: Because the brochure must accurately describe code-of-ethics and personal-trading conflict controls, the memo should explicitly flag when an exception may require disclosure cleanup or consistency checks.

step_results.build_implication: Build reviewer-editable memo drafts and remediation suggestions, but keep conclusions, tone, and any disclosure call fully in human hands.

confidence_breakdown.note: Low separation between the top two modes means this step still needs explicit reviewer judgment.

decision_rationale.why_this_mode: Assist is the right lane because the step is partially structured (52) but still carries meaningful human sign-off need (68) and liability asymmetry (71).
decision_rationale.why_not_automate: Do not let this step resolve the review on its own; it should prepare judgment, not replace it.
decision_rationale.confidence_note: Low separation between the top two modes means this step still needs explicit reviewer judgment.

review_actions: Keep reviewer confirmation prominent because the downside of a bad recommendation is still high.

step_results.step_description: The chief compliance officer decides whether the exception is a violation, a documented waiver, or a no-issue event, and assigns any sanction, disclosure, or escalation.

artifacts.name: IPO, limited-offering, and preclearance request log
Exception review memo and remediation draft

automation_fit.label: Automation fit
automation_fit.rationale: The system can organize the evidence, but the supervisory judgment about sanctions, waivers, and escalation should not be automated.

human_signoff_need.label: Human sign-off need
human_signoff_need.rationale: This is the named supervisory decision point for whether the code was violated and what the consequence should be.

liability_asymmetry.label: Liability asymmetry
liability_asymmetry.rationale: A wrong call here can create disciplinary, disclosure, and books-and-records harm in both directions.

evidence_strength.label: Evidence strength
evidence_strength.rationale: The SEC materials clearly anchor the need for review, enforcement, and retention even though they do not mechanize the disciplinary judgment itself.

evidence_notes.source_title: Final Rule: Investment Adviser Codes of Ethics
evidence_notes.claim: The adopting release expects the CCO or persons under the CCO’s authority to enforce the code and review access-person reports, which makes final adjudication an accountable human responsibility.

evidence_notes.source_title: Compliance Alert (July 2008)
evidence_notes.claim: SEC staff observed that reviewers should not self-review and that exceptions and reprimands should be documented, so tooling should package evidence but not replace the decision-maker.

evidence_notes.source_title: Investment Adviser MNPI Compliance Issues
evidence_notes.claim: The 2022 alert cites weak supervisory review and self-review failures, which is a direct reason to keep the final violation call with a named reviewer.

evidence_notes.source_title: SEC Division of Examinations Announces 2026 Priorities
evidence_notes.claim: Current examinations still test whether compliance policies are implemented and enforced, so the final sanctions or waiver decision should remain visibly owned by the accountable human reviewer.

step_results.build_implication: Keep any product surface here limited to evidence packaging, prior-decision context, and audit traceability; do not automate the actual violation or sanctions decision.

confidence_breakdown.note: Clear score separation supports the current recommendation.

decision_rationale.why_this_mode: Keep Human is mandatory because this step is an explicit supervisory approval point.
decision_rationale.why_not_automate: Automation here should only package context and evidence for the accountable reviewer.
decision_rationale.confidence_note: Clear score separation supports the current recommendation.

review_actions: Keep any future UI limited to approval support and audit traceability, not autonomous action.

step_results.step_description: Store the final decision, update the violation log, queue training or disclosure follow-up, and preserve the records needed for future exams and annual compliance review.

artifacts.name: Quarterly transaction and annual holdings package
IPO, limited-offering, and preclearance request log

automation_fit.label: Automation fit
automation_fit.rationale: Once a human decision exists, retention, certification reminders, and follow-up task creation are highly structured and repeatable.

human_signoff_need.label: Human sign-off need
human_signoff_need.rationale: The judgment has already happened; this step is mainly about complete retention and operational follow-through.

liability_asymmetry.label: Liability asymmetry
liability_asymmetry.rationale: Bad recordkeeping still matters, but the main disciplinary judgment should already be fixed before archival automation runs.

evidence_strength.label: Evidence strength
evidence_strength.rationale: The SEC sources are clear about keeping records of code violations, acknowledgments, reports, and decisions for later review.

evidence_notes.source_title: Final Rule: Investment Adviser Codes of Ethics
evidence_notes.claim: The amended recordkeeping rule requires advisers to retain the code, violations and actions taken, written acknowledgments, access-person reports, and waiver decisions, making archival mechanics highly structureable.

evidence_notes.source_title: Compliance Alert (July 2008)
evidence_notes.claim: SEC staff observed stronger programs where records, annual certifications, documented exceptions, and electronic analysis history were preserved for later review.

evidence_notes.source_title: Investment Adviser MNPI Compliance Issues
evidence_notes.claim: The 2022 alert cites firms that could not produce evidence of review or acknowledgments, which makes good retention an important control even after the substantive decision is over.

evidence_notes.source_title: SEC Division of Examinations Announces 2026 Priorities
evidence_notes.claim: Because examinations continue to assess whether compliance programs are implemented and whether annual reviews are effective, post-decision records should be easy to retrieve and trace.

step_results.build_implication: Build retention, certification, and follow-up automation after the human decision is logged so the supervisory record is exam-ready without adding new judgment risk.

confidence_breakdown.note: Clear score separation supports the current recommendation.

decision_rationale.why_this_mode: Automate is the best fit because automation fit (80) and evidence strength (82) comfortably outweigh human sign-off need (25) and liability asymmetry (30).
decision_rationale.why_not_more_human: Human review should stay focused on exceptions, not default packet handling.
decision_rationale.confidence_note: Clear score separation supports the current recommendation.

build_decisions.decision: Build automation around packet assembly, missing-report detection, and post-decision retention before expanding reviewer UI.
build_decisions.rationale: These steps are repeated monitoring mechanics with strong rule and exam anchors, and they create clean inputs for a reviewer without deciding whether conduct was a violation.

build_decisions.decision: Build reviewer aids for access-person scope checks, restricted-list and preclearance review, and remediation drafting instead of auto-calling violations.
build_decisions.rationale: These steps combine structured checks with conflict and supervisory judgment that should stay visible to a compliance reviewer.

build_decisions.decision: Do not automate final code-of-ethics violation, waiver, or sanctions decisions in slice 3.
build_decisions.rationale: The adjudication step is an accountable supervisory decision with disciplinary, disclosure, and books-and-records consequences, so automation should only package evidence for the CCO.

explicit_defers: No reviewer UI beyond this published payload
